questions.question: Explain the difference between flow control and error control in Data Link Layer protocols.
keyPoints: Flow control prevents receiver overflow by regulating sender rate
Error control handles detection and correction of corrupted/lost frames
Flow control uses mechanisms like sliding windows, error control uses ARQ
Both work together but serve distinct purposes
Flow control is about speed matching, error control is about reliability

questions.question: Calculate the channel utilization for a Stop-and-Wait protocol given: Bandwidth = 10 Mbps, Frame size = 10,000 bits, One-way propagation delay = 20 ms.
keyPoints: Transmission time tf = Frame size / Bandwidth = 10000 / 10^7 = 1 ms
Total round-trip time = tf + 2×tp = 1 + 40 = 41 ms
Utilization U = tf / (tf + 2×tp) = 1/41 ≈ 2.44%
Result shows poor utilization due to waiting time
For higher utilization, use sliding window protocols

questions.question: With a 4-bit sequence number field, compare the maximum window sizes for Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols.
keyPoints: Go-Back-N: Maximum window = 2^k - 1 = 2^4 - 1 = 15
Selective Repeat: Maximum window = 2^(k-1) = 2^3 = 8
Go-Back-N can have larger window because it uses cumulative ACKs
Selective Repeat needs smaller window to avoid ambiguity
Window size constraint in SR prevents duplicate frame acceptance

questions.question: Describe the piggybacking technique and state its advantages.
keyPoints: Piggybacking combines data and acknowledgment in a single frame
Used in bidirectional communication (full-duplex)
Reduces overhead by eliminating separate ACK frames
Improves channel utilization
Requires careful timing - ACKs must not be delayed too long

questions.question: In a Sliding Window Protocol with sender window [2,3,4,5,6], if ACK for sequence number 2 is received, what is the new sender window? Assume 3-bit sequence numbers.
keyPoints: Sequence numbers are modulo 8 (0-7) for 3 bits
Window slides by one position when ACK received
New window becomes [3,4,5,6,7]
The left edge moves from 2 to 3
New frame can now be sent with sequence number 7 (wrapping around)

questions.question: Why is Stop-and-Wait protocol inefficient for satellite links with high bandwidth-delay products?
keyPoints: Satellite links have large propagation delay (about 270ms one-way)
Stop-and-Wait allows only one frame in transit
Sender spends most time waiting for ACKs
Channel remains idle during waiting period
Example: With 270ms delay, 1ms frame = utilization < 1%

questions.question: Explain how the receiver handles out-of-order frames in Selective Repeat ARQ.
keyPoints: Receiver maintains a receive window equal to sender window
Can accept frames within the window even if out of order
Out-of-order frames are buffered (not discarded)
Cumulative ACK not used; each frame individually acknowledged
Frames are delivered to network layer in order only when contiguous

questions.question: Explain the operation of Sliding Window Protocol with Go-Back-N ARQ. Include sender and receiver operations, sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmission mechanism.
keyPoints: Sender maintains send window with lower and upper bounds
Sender can send W frames without waiting for ACKs
Each frame has sequence number from 0 to 2^k-1
Cumulative ACK: ACK N acknowledges all frames up to N-1
Sender uses single timer for oldest unacknowledged frame
When timeout occurs, retransmit all unacknowledged frames
Receiver accepts frames in order only; out-of-order discarded
Receiver window size is 1 for Go-Back-N
Explain with example: If frames 0-4 sent and frame 2 lost, receiver discards 3,4 and sender retransmits 2,3,4

questions.question: Design a flow control scheme for a Data Link Layer protocol operating on a high-speed local area network with 100 Mbps bandwidth and maximum frame size of 1500 bytes. Justify your choice of protocol parameters.
keyPoints: Choose between Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, or Selective Repeat
Calculate frame transmission time: 1500×8 / 100×10^6 = 120 μs
For LAN, propagation delay is very small (<1ms)
Even Stop-and-Wait may achieve good utilization on LANs
But sliding window provides better efficiency for larger files
Recommend Selective Repeat for better performance
Sequence number bits: With 8-bit, window up to 128
Window size calculation based on bandwidth-delay product
Explain buffer requirements at sender and receiver
Consider flow control integration with error control

questions.question: Compare and contrast Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols in terms of efficiency, buffer requirements, complexity, and suitable applications.
keyPoints: Go-Back-N: Simple receiver, sender complex with single timer
Selective Repeat: Complex both sender and receiver with multiple timers
GBN efficiency lower when errors frequent; SR better for noisy links
GBN requires receiver buffer for 1 frame; SR requires buffer for window size
GBN wastes bandwidth retransmitting good frames; SR retransmits only bad ones
Sequence number requirements: GBN 2^k-1, SR 2^(k-1)
Suitable for GBN: low error rate, small bandwidth-delay
Suitable for SR: high error rate, large bandwidth-delay products
Trade-off between complexity and efficiency
